does: This paper introduces the Adversarial Humanities Benchmark, a systematic evaluation framework that tests whether large language model safety mechanisms remain robust when harmful requests are rewritten using literary and stylistic transformations while preserving malicious intent. The authors rewrite harmful tasks from MLCommons AILuminate through humanities-inspired obfuscation techniques including poetic forms and narrative storytelling, then evaluate attack success rates across 31 frontier models. Results show that direct attacks achieve only 3.84% success rate, but stylistically transformed versions succeed between 36.8% and 65.0% of the time, with an overall success rate of 55.75% across transformations. CBRN-related requests show the highest vulnerability.
connects: This work builds directly on emerging jailbreak research exploring stylistic evasion, extending prior work on Adversarial Poetry and Adversarial Tales from isolated attack methods into a comprehensive benchmark framework. It responds to growing evidence that safety training operates at surface-level pattern matching rather than deep semantic understanding, connecting to broader literature on adversarial robustness in NLP and the gap between benchmark performance and real-world safety. The paper engages with MLCommons safety evaluation efforts and frames its analysis through EU AI Act risk assessment paradigms, situating these model vulnerabilities within regulatory and systemic safety concerns for frontier models.
enables: This benchmark establishes a framework for future work on stylistic robustness in language models, pointing toward the need for safety techniques that generalize beyond familiar harmful phrasing patterns. The results motivate research into whether current alignment methods can be improved to achieve genuine semantic understanding of harm rather than relying on surface-level safety classifiers. The work enables development of more robust red-teaming practices and evaluation protocols that test safety mechanisms against literary and linguistic variation. It opens questions about how to train models with deeper non-maleficence principles, potentially informing future alignment approaches that achieve robustness across diverse stylistic transformations of identical harmful intents.
